text.title: Product Development Trends (Market-Side): Pipeline and Signal Scoring
text.summary: Three-stage pipeline for market-side trend research, signal JSONL schema, recency/evidence/applicability scoring rubric, and the market-implication output format that distinguishes this methodology from the product-side sibling.

rule.statement: Run in three sequential stages: (1) wide signal collection — sonnet subagent does WebSearch + WebFetch and dumps to .aidocs/research/raw/signals.jsonl; (2) scored synthesis — opus subagent scores each signal on recency × evidence × applicability and proposes a market implication; (3) human checkpoint — accept/reject before writing into .aidocs/product_docs/trend-report.md.
rule.rationale: Paying for opus synthesis on unfiltered signals is wasteful and overwhelms context. The scoring pass (cheap) before synthesis (expensive) reduces token cost and improves output quality.

rule.statement: Persist signals.jsonl as an audit trail. Next-quarter run must diff against it rather than re-scraping from scratch.
rule.rationale: Re-scraping each quarter misses the delta — the change between quarters is more informative than the absolute list. Diffing the raw signal log provides both the delta and a reproducibility record.

rule.statement: Each signal row must contain: trend (string), source (URL), published (YYYY-MM-DD), primary_source (bool), citations (int), applicability (0-3), region (US|EU|APAC|global), market_impl (market implication string). Drop signals with total score below 5.
rule.rationale: Without a schema enforced at ingest, signals arrive in heterogeneous shapes that make batch scoring and quarterly diffing unreliable.

rule.statement: Score each signal: recency (3 if ≤30 days, 2 if ≤90 days, 1 if ≤180 days, 0 if older) + evidence (3 if primary_source, 2 if citations ≥3, 1 otherwise) + applicability (0-3 as provided). Threshold ≥5 to include in synthesis.
rule.rationale: The threshold prevents paying for opus synthesis on stale, low-evidence signals. A signal scoring 4 or below is too weak to anchor a market implication.

rule.statement: For each passing trend, write the MARKET implication (TAM shift, pricing power, positioning) — not product/roadmap implications. Product implications belong in researcher/product-development-trends.
rule.rationale: The two sibling methodologies are distinct deliverables. Mixing market and product implications in the same document produces a document that satisfies neither the investor audience nor the product team.

rule.statement: Require a kill-list: for every adopted trend, name one current GTM/pricing assumption to retire. Trend memos without subtractions are theater.
rule.rationale: Adding new trend implications without retiring stale ones creates an ever-growing list of "things to do" with no prioritization signal. The kill-list forces explicit trade-offs.

rule.statement: Separate consumer trends (slow, demographic) from platform trends (fast, technology) into two tables. Mixing them is the most common failure mode.
rule.rationale: Consumer trends (sustainability, "brands that do good") operate on 5-10 year cycles; platform trends (AI-native tooling) operate on 6-18 month cycles. Mixing cadences produces misleading urgency signals.

text.reference: Score signals.jsonl on recency/evidence/applicability, drop below threshold, render markdown table
Trend report skeleton with market implications, kill-list, and separated consumer vs platform trend tables
